***Transformers: Dark of the Moon*** is a 2011 American [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") based on [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro")'s *[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(toy_line) "Transformers (toy line)")* toy line. It is the sequel to *[Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen](/wiki/Transformers:_Revenge_of_the_Fallen "Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen")* (2009) and the third installment in the [*Transformers* film series](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series)#Films "Transformers (film series)"). Like its predecessor, the film was directed by [Michael Bay](/wiki/Michael_Bay "Michael Bay"), and written by [Ehren Kruger](/wiki/Ehren_Kruger "Ehren Kruger"). [Shia LaBeouf](/wiki/Shia_LaBeouf "Shia LaBeouf"), [Josh Duhamel](/wiki/Josh_Duhamel "Josh Duhamel"), [John Turturro](/wiki/John_Turturro "John Turturro"), [Tyrese Gibson](/wiki/Tyrese_Gibson "Tyrese Gibson"), [Kevin Dunn](/wiki/Kevin_Dunn "Kevin Dunn"), and [Julie White](/wiki/Julie_White "Julie White") reprise their roles from the previous films, with [Rosie Huntington-Whiteley](/wiki/Rosie_Huntington-Whiteley "Rosie Huntington-Whiteley"), [Patrick Dempsey](/wiki/Patrick_Dempsey "Patrick Dempsey"), [John Malkovich](/wiki/John_Malkovich "John Malkovich"), and [Frances McDormand](/wiki/Frances_McDormand "Frances McDormand") joining the cast. In the film, [Optimus Prime](/wiki/Optimus_Prime#Transformers_(film_series) "Optimus Prime") and [Sam Witwicky](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Sam_Witwicky "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") must lead the [Autobots](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Autobots "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") against [Megatron](/wiki/Megatron#Transformers_Cinematic_Universe "Megatron") and the [Decepticons](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Decepticons "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") as they battle to possess powerful technology abandoned on the Moon in order to restore Cybertron.

Development of a third *Transformers* film began in May 2007. The film employed both regular [35 mm film](/wiki/35_mm_movie_film "35 mm movie film") cameras and specially developed [3D](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") cameras, with filming locations in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), Moscow, and Washington, D.C. The visual effects involved more complex robots than the previous films, which took longer to render. The release date was moved from July 1 to June 29, in order to monitor an early response to footage.

Early screenings took place on June 28, 2011, one night before worldwide release by [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"). The film received mixed reviews from critics and grossed $1.124 billion worldwide, becoming the [fifth highest-grossing film in history](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films "List of highest-grossing films") at the time, and [the second highest-grossing film of 2011](/wiki/2011_in_film#Highest-grossing_films "2011 in film"). Like [the first film](/wiki/Transformers_(film) "Transformers (film)"), it was nominated for [Best Sound Editing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Sound_Editing "Academy Award for Best Sound Editing"), [Best Sound Mixing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Sound_Mixing "Academy Award for Best Sound Mixing"), and [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") at the [Academy Awards](/wiki/84th_Academy_Awards "84th Academy Awards"). It was followed by *[Transformers: Age of Extinction](/wiki/Transformers:_Age_of_Extinction "Transformers: Age of Extinction")* in 2014.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

The *Ark* spacecraft escapes from the planet [Cybertron](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Cybertron "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") carrying an invention capable of ending the war between [Autobots](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Autobots "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") and [Decepticons](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Decepticons "List of Transformers film series cast and characters"), and crash-lands on the [dark side of Earth's Moon](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon") in 1962. [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") detects the crash, and President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") authorizes a mission to put a man on the Moon as a cover for investigating the spacecraft. In 1969, the crew of [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11_in_popular_culture "Apollo 11 in popular culture") lands on the Moon and secretly inspects the *Ark* before returning to Earth.

In the present day, three years after the battle of Egypt,[[b]](#cite_note-9) the Autobots assist humanity in preventing major conflicts. During a mission to the site of the [1986 Chernobyl nuclear disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") to investigate suspected alien technology, the Autobots are attacked by Decepticon scientist [Shockwave](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Shockwave "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") and his giant worm Driller. After the two escape, [Optimus Prime](/wiki/Optimus_Prime#Transformers_(film_series) "Optimus Prime") discovers that the technology is a [fuel cell](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") from the *Ark*. The Autobots travel to the Moon and discover [Sentinel Prime](/wiki/Sentinel_Prime#Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon "Sentinel Prime"), the Autobots' leader before Optimus, in a comatose state along with five Pillars he created as a means of establishing a "Space Bridge", a [wormhole](/wiki/Wormhole "Wormhole") able to teleport matter between two points. On Earth, Optimus uses the energy of the [Matrix of Leadership](/wiki/Matrix_of_Leadership "Matrix of Leadership") to revive Sentinel.

Meanwhile, [Sam Witwicky](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Sam_Witwicky "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") lives with his new girlfriend, [Carly Spencer](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Carly_Spencer "List of Transformers film series cast and characters"), but is unable to work with the Autobots. At his new job, co-worker Jerry Wang gives him information about the *Ark*, before being assassinated by the Decepticon [Laserbeak](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Laserbeak "List of Transformers film series cast and characters"). Sam contacts Seymour Simmons to investigate the Decepticons' murders of people connected to the American and [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") lunar space missions. They locate two surviving cosmonauts, who show photos of hundreds of Pillars on the Moon, and realize that the Decepticons raided the *Ark* long before the Apollo 11 mission and left Sentinel and the five Pillars for the Autobots to find, knowing that Sentinel was the key to activating the Pillars. Meanwhile, Sam and the Autobots fight off three pursuing Decepticons — [Hatchet](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Hatchet "List of Transformers film series cast and characters"), [Crankcase](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Crankcase "List of Transformers film series cast and characters"), and [Crowbar](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Crowbar "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") — and return Sentinel to their base. Revealing that he made a deal with [Megatron](/wiki/Megatron#Transformers_Cinematic_Universe "Megatron") to ensure Cybertron's survival, Sentinel betrays the Autobots and kills [Ironhide](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#Ironhide "List of Transformers film series cast and characters").

Sentinel uses the Pillars to transport hundreds of concealed Decepticons from the Moon to Earth. [Dylan Gould](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters "List of Transformers film series cast and characters"), Carly's boss, is revealed to be working with the Decepticons, and captures Carly with the help of [Soundwave](/wiki/Soundwave_(Transformers)#Transformers_Cinematic_Universe "Soundwave (Transformers)"), who was disguised as Carly's car. At the demand of the Decepticons, the Autobots are exiled from Earth, but [Starscream](/wiki/Starscream#Transformers_Cinematic_Universe "Starscream") destroys their ship as it takes off. The Decepticons invade [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") while placing Pillars around the world to begin transporting Cybertron to the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), planning to enslave humanity and use Earth's resources to rebuild their homeworld. Sam teams up with former [NEST](/wiki/List_of_Transformers_film_series_cast_and_characters#NEST "List of Transformers film series cast and characters") soldier Robert Epps to go into Chicago to save Carly and arrest Dylan. They are nearly killed by the Decepticons before the Autobots, who survived their ship's destruction, intervene. Sam, along with NEST teams led by Lennox and [Navy SEALs](/wiki/United_States_Navy_SEALs "United States Navy SEALs"), rescue Carly and begin fighting the Decepticons. During the battle, the Autobot Que is executed, and [Bumblebee](/wiki/Bumblebee_(Transformers)#Transformers_(film_series) "Bumblebee (Transformers)") kills Soundwave, while Optimus fights Sentinel. Laserbeak, Starscream, Shockwave, and the Driller are also killed in the ensuing battle. Meanwhile, Wheelie and Brains sneak onboard and sabotage the Decepticon mothership.

Carly convinces Megatron that he will be replaced by Sentinel as leader of the Decepticons. Sam fights Dylan and knocks him into the Pillar, which electrocutes and kills him. Bumblebee destroys the Control Pillar, closing the Bridge and causing the partially transported Cybertron to implode. Sentinel overpowers Optimus, but is incapacitated by Megatron, who is determined to regain his leadership. Megatron offers a truce, but Optimus refuses and kills Megatron by tearing off his head, before executing Sentinel for his betrayal. Sam and Carly are reunited, and the Autobots remain on Earth as their hopeful home.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Development")]

> You just learn a lot more about the hierarchy, and there's more about the history of what they had in Cybertron. Leonard Nimoy plays a great role.

— Michael Bay, on developing Sentinel Prime's character[[9]](#cite_note-11)

Before the release of *[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(film) "Transformers (film)")* (2007), [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") began developing two sequels.[[10]](#cite_note-12) As a preemptive measure before the release of *[Revenge of the Fallen](/wiki/Transformers:_Revenge_of_the_Fallen "Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen")*, Michael Lucchi and Paramount announced on March 16, 2009, that a third film would be released on July 1, 2011, which earned a surprised response from director [Michael Bay](/wiki/Michael_Bay "Michael Bay"): "I said I was taking off a year from *Transformers*. Paramount made a mistake in dating *Transformers 3*—they asked me on the phone—I said yes to July 1—but for 2012—whoops! Not 2011! That would mean I would have to start prep in September. No way. My brain needs a break from fighting robots."[[11]](#cite_note-13) Screenwriters [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci") and [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman"), who had worked on the first two *Transformers* films, declined to return for the third film, with Kurtzman declaring that "the franchise is so wonderful that it deserves to be fresh, all the time. We just felt like we'd given it a lot and didn't have an insight for where to go with it next".[[12]](#cite_note-14) *Revenge of the Fallen*'s co-writer [Ehren Kruger](/wiki/Ehren_Kruger "Ehren Kruger") became the only screenwriter for *Dark of the Moon*. Kruger had frequent meetings with [Industrial Light & Magic](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic")'s (ILM) [visual effects](/wiki/Visual_effects "Visual effects") producers, who suggested plot points such as the scenes in Chernobyl.[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15) Additionally writer [Jenni Konner](/wiki/Jenni_Konner "Jenni Konner") was brought on to help punch up the script, fleshing out the female characters, and adding humorous jokes.[[14]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17)

On October 1, 2009, Bay revealed that *Dark of the Moon* had already gone into pre-production and its planned release returned to its originally intended date from 2012 to July 1, 2011.[[16]](#cite_note-18) Due to the revived interest in [3D filming](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") technology brought on by the success of *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*,[[17]](#cite_note-19) talks between Paramount, ILM, and Bay had considered the possibility of the next *Transformers* film being filmed in 3D, and testing was performed to bring the technology into Bay's work.[[18]](#cite_note-20) Bay originally was not much interested in the format as he felt it did not fit his "aggressive style" of filmmaking, but he was convinced after talks with *Avatar* director [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron"),[[19]](#cite_note-usatoday-21) who even offered the technical crew from that film. Cameron reportedly told Bay about 3D, "You gotta look at it as a toy, it's another fun tool to help get emotion and character and create an experience."[[20]](#cite_note-CameronBay-22) Bay was reluctant to shoot with 3D cameras since in test he found them to be too cumbersome for his filming style, but he did not want to implement the technology in post-production either since he was not pleased with the results.[[21]](#cite_note-23) In addition to using the 3D Fusion camera rigs developed by Cameron's team,[[20]](#cite_note-CameronBay-22) Bay and the team spent nine months developing a more portable 3D camera that could be brought into location.[[19]](#cite_note-usatoday-21)

In a hidden extra for the [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") version of *Revenge of the Fallen*, Bay expressed his intention to make *Transformers 3* not necessarily larger than *Revenge of the Fallen*, but deeper into the mythology, for it to get more character development and for it to be darker and more serious. [Unicron](/wiki/Unicron "Unicron") is briefly shown in a secret *Transformers 3* preview feature in the *Revenge of the Fallen* Blu-ray disc. Ultimately, the producers decided to forgo a plot involving the planet-eating Transformer, and no further comments were ever made on the subject.[[22]](#cite_note-blurayvideo-24) Having been called *Transformers 3* up to that point, the film's final title was officially revealed to be *Dark of the Moon* in October 2010.[[23]](#cite_note-25) After *Revenge of the Fallen* was almost universally panned by critics and audiences, Bay acknowledged the general flaws of the script, having blamed the [2007-08 Writers strike](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Writers_Guild_of_America_strike "2007–08 Writers Guild of America strike") before the film for many problems. Bay also said the film will get rid of the "dorky comedy" from the last film.[[24]](#cite_note-26) On March 19, 2010, the script was said to be completed.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Casting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Patrick_Dempsey_Transformers_3_set.jpg/250px-Patrick_Dempsey_Transformers_3_set.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrick_Dempsey_Transformers_3_set.jpg)

Dempsey on the set of *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* in 2010

[Megan Fox](/wiki/Megan_Fox "Megan Fox") was originally attached to the film, and [Patrick Dempsey](/wiki/Patrick_Dempsey "Patrick Dempsey")'s role as [Dylan Gould](/wiki/Dylan_Gould "Dylan Gould") was to be the employer of Fox's character, [Mikaela Banes](/wiki/Mikaela_Banes "Mikaela Banes").[[26]](#cite_note-28) According to various published sources, Fox's absence from the film was due to executive producer Steven Spielberg ultimately choosing not to renew her role in light of her comparing Bay and his work ethic to [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"),[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-LaBeouf-30) although representatives for the actress said that it was her decision to leave the film franchise.[[29]](#cite_note-31) Bay later claimed that executive producer [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") told him to fire Fox,[[30]](#cite_note-gq.com-32) a claim that Spielberg denied.[[31]](#cite_note-33) "I wasn't hurt", Bay stated, "because I know that's just Megan. Megan loves to get a response. And she does it in kind of the wrong way. I'm sorry, Megan. I'm sorry I made you work twelve hours. I'm sorry that I'm making you show up on time. Movies are not always warm and fuzzy."[[30]](#cite_note-gq.com-32)

With Fox not reprising her role, [Rosie Huntington-Whiteley](/wiki/Rosie_Huntington-Whiteley "Rosie Huntington-Whiteley") was chosen to play Sam's new girlfriend.[[32]](#cite_note-34) [Ramón Rodríguez](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Rodr%C3%ADguez_(actor) "Ramón Rodríguez (actor)") was initially planned to be in the film, in a role bigger than the one he had in *Revenge of the Fallen*, but he was dropped during early production.[[33]](#cite_note-35) A few well-known actors such as [John Malkovich](/wiki/John_Malkovich "John Malkovich") and [Frances McDormand](/wiki/Frances_McDormand "Frances McDormand") were also selected for roles in the film. Malkovich explains: "I play a guy called Bruce Brazos, who's just a loudmouth, kind of business man who's Shia's character's boss. Who's just a jerk, and a kind of a loud one. But a fun character. Nice. It was fun. Very, very enjoyable, just with Shia, Rosie a little bit, and with John Turturro. So, for me, it was a blast."[[34]](#cite_note-36) Another well-known actor, [Ken Jeong](/wiki/Ken_Jeong "Ken Jeong"), was cast as an eccentric co-worker and stalker. Jeong described the film, "Yeah, it's a small role in *Transformers*, but yeah. I had an out-of-body experience working on that one because I just couldn't believe I was there. Still, that was not a thing where oh, I'm going to be a part of a blockbuster franchise like *Transformers 3* or even now *[Hangover 2](/wiki/The_Hangover_Part_II "The Hangover Part II")* for that matter. So I can't believe I'm a part of these franchises in any way. It was amazing. Michael Bay is brilliant and it'll blow your mind."[[35]](#cite_note-37)

For the role of Decepticon scientist Shockwave, veteran *Transformers* voice actor [Corey Burton](/wiki/Corey_Burton "Corey Burton") was originally approached to voice the character after previously doing so in [the original television series](/wiki/The_Transformers_(TV_series) "The Transformers (TV series)") and *[Transformers: Animated](/wiki/Transformers:_Animated "Transformers: Animated")*. Burton declined the offer, citing his work schedule and disinterest in being involved in a blockbuster franchise. [David Warner](/wiki/David_Warner_(actor) "David Warner (actor)") (Burton's influence on his Shockwave voice) was briefly considered, but the role was ultimately given to [Frank Welker](/wiki/Frank_Welker "Frank Welker"), adding to his already long list of *Transformers* characters.[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)

### Themes and inspirations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Themes and inspirations")]

Skids and Mudflap were omitted from *Dark of the Moon* in response to *Revenge of the Fallen*'s negative reception and the characters' "dorky comedy", which Bay realized that they formed "a final crescendo".[[38]](#cite_note-TwinsGone-40) Despite fan rumors that Skids and Mudflap would appear in the film, Bay made a public $25,000 "bet" that the characters would not be seen in *Dark of the Moon*.[[38]](#cite_note-TwinsGone-40) They had a brief cameo in the N.E.S.T headquarters in their vehicle modes in the film.

Bay acknowledged that *Revenge of the Fallen* was "disappointing to the fans" and said that he "doesn't want the third one to suck".[[39]](#cite_note-BlackHawkDown-41) Bay said that he wanted *Dark of the Moon*'s final battle to be more geographic and feature a "small group of heroes" like [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott")'s war–drama *[Black Hawk Down](/wiki/Black_Hawk_Down_(film) "Black Hawk Down (film)")*, [Joe Dante](/wiki/Joe_Dante "Joe Dante")'s science fiction *[Small Soldiers](/wiki/Small_Soldiers "Small Soldiers")* and [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro")'s *[G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra](/wiki/G.I._Joe:_The_Rise_of_Cobra "G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra")*.[[39]](#cite_note-BlackHawkDown-41) Bay also decided to include [Shockwave](/wiki/Shockwave_(Transformers) "Shockwave (Transformers)") because he considers the character "bad" and "He's got a much bigger gun [than Megatron and is] a little bit more vicious."[[39]](#cite_note-BlackHawkDown-41) According to [The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club"), the film had several minor story inspirations from the 1980s cartoon *[The Transformers](/wiki/The_Transformers_(TV_series) "The Transformers (TV series)")*, including the usage of a Space Bridge, and the "kicking the Autobots out".[[40]](#cite_note-42)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Similar to the previous two installments, the film was told in the human point-of-view to engage the audience. Bay wanted Sam to have a girlfriend like in the first two films. Actor Shia LaBeouf said that Sam and Mikaela had become "one character" and, although he would "miss" Megan Fox, with this change "you have discovery again from a new perspective."[[41]](#cite_note-Breznican-43) LaBeouf also stated that the additions of Huntington-Whiteley and new characters allows *Dark of the Moon* to keep the "magic" of the first film.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

*Dark of the Moon* also had numerous *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")* references, partly because scriptwriter Ehren Kruger was a "big Star Trek geek",[[43]](#cite_note-StarTrekRef-45) but also as a nod to the fact that new character Sentinel Prime was voiced by [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy"), who originated the iconic role of [Spock](/wiki/Spock "Spock") on *Star Trek* and voiced [Galvatron](/wiki/Galvatron "Galvatron") in *[The Transformers: The Movie](/wiki/The_Transformers:_The_Movie "The Transformers: The Movie")*. The first *Star Trek* reference is when refugee robots Brains and Wheelie, who live in Sam and Carly's apartment complex, are watching *[TOS](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series "Star Trek: The Original Series")* episode "[Amok Time](/wiki/Amok_Time "Amok Time")"; Wheelie comments "I've seen this one. It's the one where Spock goes nuts."[[43]](#cite_note-StarTrekRef-45) The second reference is when Sam meets his girlfriend, Carly Spencer at work, and is being introduced to Carly's employer, Dylan Gould, Sam marvels at their workplace: "It's a beautiful building you guys have. Like the [Starship *Enterprise*](/wiki/Starship_Enterprise "Starship Enterprise") in here."[[43]](#cite_note-StarTrekRef-45) The third reference is when Bumblebee says goodbye to Sam at Cape Canaveral: the words "my friend" are sampled from Spock in *[Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan](/wiki/Star_Trek_II:_The_Wrath_of_Khan "Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan")* ("You are ... my friend. I am and always shall be yours.").[[43]](#cite_note-StarTrekRef-45) The fourth and final reference is when Sentinel Prime activates the Control Pillar, quoting Spock's maxim in *Star Trek II* and *[Star Trek III: The Search for Spock](/wiki/Star_Trek_III:_The_Search_for_Spock "Star Trek III: The Search for Spock")*, "The needs of the many outweigh the needs of the few".[[43]](#cite_note-StarTrekRef-45)

### Filming

Its production cost was reported $195 million, with the cost of the 3D filming accounting at $30 million of the budget.[[6]](#cite_note-Fernandez-7) Preparation for filming began on April 7, 2010, in [Northwest Indiana](/wiki/Northwest_Indiana "Northwest Indiana"), specifically around [Gary](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana"), which played [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") in the film.[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)[[46]](#cite_note-48) [Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") commenced on May 18, 2010, with shooting locations including [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), and [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow").[[47]](#cite_note-49)[[48]](#cite_note-50) The first six weeks were spent in Los Angeles: locations included [Sherman Oaks](/wiki/Sherman_Oaks,_Los_Angeles "Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles"), Fourth Avenue, and 5. Main. The next four weeks were spent in Chicago. Locations filmed in Chicago included [LaSalle Street](/wiki/LaSalle_Street "LaSalle Street"), [Michigan Avenue](/wiki/Michigan_Avenue_(Chicago) "Michigan Avenue (Chicago)"), Bacino's of Lincoln Park at 2204 North Lincoln Avenue and areas surrounding the [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower "Willis Tower").[[49]](#cite_note-51) The scenes set in Michigan Ave featured a substantial amount of pyrotechnics and stunt work.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Filming in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") was planned to take place in August but the Chicago shoot was extended until September 1.[[51]](#cite_note-53) In late September, the production moved to [NASA's](/wiki/NASA "NASA") [Kennedy Space Center](/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center "Kennedy Space Center") in Florida, just before the launch of [Space Shuttle](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle") mission [STS-133](/wiki/STS-133 "STS-133").[[52]](#cite_note-54) Scenes were filmed at [Launch Pad 39A](/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center_Launch_Complex_39A "Kennedy Space Center Launch Complex 39A"), the [Vehicle Assembly Building](/wiki/Vehicle_Assembly_Building "Vehicle Assembly Building") and the [Orbiter Processing Facility](/wiki/Orbiter_Processing_Facility "Orbiter Processing Facility").[[53]](#cite_note-55)

[![Four men wearing battle fatigues and holding guns and a crewmember wearing a white shirt, a hat and a walkie-talkie attached to its shorts stand on a set resembling a war torn city.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Transformers3ActorsSetJuly10.jpg/250px-Transformers3ActorsSetJuly10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transformers3ActorsSetJuly10.jpg)

Actors on a *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* set in July 2010

While filming in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), the crew shot on the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall") and Bay said that there would be a car race on the location. Two further locations announced were the [Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum") and the former [Tower Automotive](/wiki/Tower_International "Tower International") complex on [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee")'s north side, then under redevelopment for mixed use also the city's equipment yard. Filming was scheduled to take place there after work was done in Chicago.[[54]](#cite_note-56) On September 23, scenes were filmed at the former city hall in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). On October 16, a flashback scene that takes place in the later 1960s was shot at the [Johnson Space Center](/wiki/Johnson_Space_Center "Johnson Space Center") in [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), using extras with period fashion and hairstyles.[[55]](#cite_note-57) A day of shooting was also spent at the [Angkor Wat](/wiki/Angkor_Wat "Angkor Wat") temple complex in [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia").[[56]](#cite_note-58) Other planned filming locations included Africa and China.[[57]](#cite_note-bayupdate-59) Though about 70% of the film's live-action footage was shot in 3D[[58]](#cite_note-collider-60) using [Arri Alexa](/wiki/Arri_Alexa "Arri Alexa") and Sony F35 cameras,[[59]](#cite_note-61) more than half of the film still had to be [converted into 3D](/wiki/2D_to_3D_conversion "2D to 3D conversion") in post production to fix technical flaws that 3D filming produces.[[60]](#cite_note-62) Other footage that needed to be converted into 3D in post production was either completely computer-generated imagery or shot in the [anamorphic format](/wiki/Anamorphic_format "Anamorphic format") on [35 mm film](/wiki/35_mm_movie_film "35 mm movie film").[[58]](#cite_note-collider-60) 35 mm film was used for scenes filmed in [slow motion](/wiki/Slow_motion "Slow motion") and scenes such as closeups of faces or shots of the sky that required higher image quality than the [HD digital](/wiki/Digital_cinematography "Digital cinematography") 3D cameras could provide.[[58]](#cite_note-collider-60) 35 mm cameras were also used for scenes where the 3D cameras proved to be too heavy, or were subject to [strobing](/wiki/Jerkiness "Jerkiness") or electrical damage from dust.[[58]](#cite_note-collider-60) Principal photography officially concluded on November 9, 2010.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

*Dark of the Moon* was found to carry recycled footage from an earlier film directed by Michael Bay—*[The Island](/wiki/The_Island_(2005_film) "The Island (2005 film)")* (2005).[[62]](#cite_note-64) Bay recycled the same footage from his film *[Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor_(film) "Pearl Harbor (film)")* (2001) in the first *[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(2007_film) "Transformers (2007 film)") film*.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

### Accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Accidents")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Transformer3B.jpg/250px-Transformer3B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transformer3B.jpg)

Shooting of the duel between Sam Witwicky (Shia LaBeouf) and Dylan Gould (Patrick Dempsey) in Chicago.

Filming was delayed on September 2, 2010, when an extra was seriously injured during a stunt in [Hammond](/wiki/Hammond,_Indiana "Hammond, Indiana"), Indiana. Due to a failed weld, a steel cable snapped from a car being towed and hit the extra's car, damaging her skull. The extra, identified as Gabriela Cedillo, had to undergo brain surgery.[[64]](#cite_note-66) The injury has left her permanently brain-damaged, paralyzed on her left side and her left eye stitched shut.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) Paramount admitted responsibility for the accident and covered all of Cedillo's medical costs.[[67]](#cite_note-69) Nevertheless, Cedillo's family filed a lawsuit on October 5, citing seven counts of negligence against Paramount, and several other defendants (not including Bay), with total damages sought in excess of $350,000.[[68]](#cite_note-70) Cedillo's attorney, Todd Smith, said, "This was an attractive 24-year-old girl who had dreams and aspirations involving acting, and this kind of injury may well have a serious impact on her dreams."[[69]](#cite_note-CedilloAccident-71) The filed complaint reads that "Cedillo has endured and will in the future endure pain and suffering; has become disfigured and disabled; has suffered a loss of the enjoyment of a normal life; has been damaged in her capacity to earn a living; has incurred and will in the future incur expenses for medical services, all of which are permanent in nature."[[69]](#cite_note-CedilloAccident-71) In response to the suit, Paramount released the following statement: "We are all terribly sorry that this accident occurred. Our thoughts, prayers and best wishes are with Gabriela, her family and loved ones. The production will continue to provide all the help we can to Gabriela and her family during this difficult time."[[69]](#cite_note-CedilloAccident-71) In May 2012, it was revealed that an $18 million settlement had been reached between Paramount and the Cedillo family.[[70]](#cite_note-72) Indiana [OSHA](/wiki/OSHA "OSHA")'s investigation was criticised.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

A second accident occurred on October 11, 2010, in Washington, D.C. While filming a chase scene at 3rd Street and Maryland Avenue, SW, a Metropolitan Police K9 unit SUV was accidentally rammed by the Camaro that portrays Bumblebee in the film. The area had been closed off by the Washington, D.C., police, and it is unclear why the SUV was there. Both drivers were uninjured,[[72]](#cite_note-74) but the Camaro was severely damaged.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Reports indicate that the K9 unit was heading to a report of a bomb threat as part of a Bomb Squad response, but was not using the same radio frequency as the units guarding the filming and did not realize it had gone the wrong way until it collided with the Bumblebee Camaro. Many fans who witnessed the crash were horrified at the damage the Bumblebee Camaro suffered, and cast and crew members reacted quickly to cover up the damaged Camaro and secure the scene.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

As with the previous *Transformers* installments, [Industrial Light & Magic](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic") (ILM) was the main CGI visual effects company for *Dark of the Moon*. ILM had been working on the pre-visualization for six months before principal photography started, resulting in 20 minutes' worth of footage.[[75]](#cite_note-ILMpanel-77) [Digital Domain](/wiki/Digital_Domain "Digital Domain") also rendered 350 CGI shots, including the characters Laserbeak, Brains, Wheelie, and the Decepticon protoforms concealed on the Moon, the space bridge, and a [skydiving](/wiki/Skydiving "Skydiving") sequence.[[76]](#cite_note-78)[[77]](#cite_note-79)

| External audio | |
| --- | --- |
| Industrial Light & Magic's interview for the effects of the film. | |
| [audio icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_arts.svg) [Listen to the Interview](https://archive.org/download/Transformers3IlmPanelInterview/IlmPanelInterview.mp3) | |

ILM's visual effects supervisor [Scott Farrar](/wiki/Scott_Farrar "Scott Farrar") said that "not only were the film's effects ambitious, they also had to be designed for 3D", and explained the company's solutions for the new perspective: "We did make sure things are as bright as possible; Michael called up theatre owners to make sure they keep the lamps bright in the theatres ... make everything a little sharper, because we know that through the steps, no matter what, when you get to the final screening things tend to go less sharp."[[78]](#cite_note-HollywoodReporter5things-80) On the last weekend of ILM's work on *Dark of the Moon*, the company's entire [render farm](/wiki/Render_farm "Render farm") was being used for the film, giving ILM more than 200,000 hours of rendering power a day—or equivalent to 22.8 years of rendering time in a 24-hour period.[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15) Farrar embraced the detail in creating giant robots for 3D, making sure that in close-ups of the Transformers' faces "you see all the details in the nooks and crannies of these pieces. It's totally unlike a plain surface subject like a human head or an animated head." The supervisor said that Bay's style of cinematography helped integrate the robots into the scenes, as "Michael is keen on having foreground/midground/background depth in his shots, even in normal live-action shots. He'll say, 'Put some stuff hanging here!' It could be women's stockings or forks and knives dangling from a string out of focus—it doesn't matter, but it gives you depth, and focus depth, and makes it more interesting."[[79]](#cite_note-ILM3-D-81)

[![In a city full of skyscrapers, a robot wearing jet thrusters on his back holding a cannon flies toward a large snake-like robot. A tentacle of the larger robot is exploding.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/fb/DrillerDOTM.jpg/330px-DrillerDOTM.jpg)](/wiki/File:DrillerDOTM.jpg)

Considerable digital animation was required for the elaborate Driller as it comprised over 70,000 parts, significantly more than Optimus Prime's 10,000 parts.

The most complicated effects involved the "Driller", a giant snake-like creature with an eel-like body and spinning rotator blades, knives and teeth. In *Revenge of the Fallen*, it took 72 hours per frame to fully [render](/wiki/3-D_rendering "3-D rendering") Devastator for the IMAX format, which is approximately a frame amount of 4,000. For the Driller, which required the entire render farm, it was up to 122 hours per frame.[[75]](#cite_note-ILMpanel-77) The most complex scene involved the Driller destroying a computer-generated [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper"), which took 288 hours per frame.[[78]](#cite_note-HollywoodReporter5things-80) For said sequence, ILM relied on its internal proprietary physics simulation engine to depict the destruction of the building, which included breaking concrete floors and walls, windows, columns and pieces of office furnishings. ILM digital production supervisor Nigel Sumner explained: "We did a lot of tests early on to figure out how to break the building apart, exploring a lot of the procedural options. A building that's 70 feet tall—to go in and hand-score the geometry so when it fractures or falls apart—would be a time-consuming, laborious process. The floor of a building may be made of concrete. How does concrete fracture when it tears apart? The pillars would be made of a similar material but made of rebar or other engineering components. We'd look at how a building would blow apart and then choose the best tool to help achieve the properties of that during a simulation."[[79]](#cite_note-ILM3-D-81)

[![A large red and gray robot, with humanoid shape](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/0d/DOTMSentinelPrime.jpg/250px-DOTMSentinelPrime.jpg)](/wiki/File:DOTMSentinelPrime.jpg)

[![A black and white photo of actor Sean Connery, with many balloons and two persons in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Sean_Connery_1980.jpg/250px-Sean_Connery_1980.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sean_Connery_1980.jpg)

Sentinel Prime (left) was modelled after Actor [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery") (right).[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15)

The scenes in Chicago were mostly shot on location, as Bay believed the plates had to really be shot in the real city. Farrar was always fascinated with the idea of shooting on location and then blending the film with computer-generated imagery. He said: "We try and shoot everything real. You may have seen some films recently where the entire city has been destroyed and it's entirely CGI. Well, for a *Transformers* film, it's different because we actually went to Chicago. If you start with the real thing, you have a lot more to work with to make it look real. So for a couple of months there, I was in a helicopter shooting aerial plates of the real buildings. And we'd add destruction to all the backgrounds – smoke, fire, debris, fighter planes, war, battles, torn up streets – to real cityscapes."[[79]](#cite_note-ILM3-D-81) Four ILM employees also traveled to Chicago and photographed buildings from top to bottom at six different times of the day in order to create a digital model of the city to be used in certain scenes.[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15) ILM's crew designed many major action scenes,[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15) with many of the Chicago battle concepts coming from the helicopter shooting of the aerial plates.[[75]](#cite_note-ILMpanel-77)

Animator Scott Benza said Sentinel Prime had a face "more human-like than any of the other robots", with a more complex frame and "a greater number of plates" so it could be more expressive.[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15) ILM had based most of Sentinel Prime's features on [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery"), and after Leonard Nimoy was cast to voice the role, the effects were altered to incorporate Nimoy's acting as well. Every robot would take approximately 30 weeks to build visually. Originally, the fight between Sentinel Prime, Optimus Prime, and Megatron was considered to be on water in the [Chicago River](/wiki/Chicago_River "Chicago River"), but the budget was cut and ILM realized that they would not want to present that version of the final battle to Bay, so they decided to have the battle take place on the bridge over the river.[[75]](#cite_note-ILMpanel-77) For a sequence where Bumblebee catches Sam and Lennox while transforming in mid-air, a digital double of Shia LaBeouf was combined with footage of the actor in high speed so that the effects team could time it for slow-motion.[[79]](#cite_note-ILM3-D-81)

Since Bay shoots all his films in [anamorphic](/wiki/Anamorphic "Anamorphic") format, *Dark of the Moon*'s representation would be "squeezed in" to distort the image, and ILM would add in the robots and "un-distort" the image.[[75]](#cite_note-ILMpanel-77) The ample variety of filming formats used—single camera, 3D [stereo](/wiki/Stereoscopy "Stereoscopy") rigs with two cameras, anamorphic and [spherical lenses](/wiki/Spherical_lens "Spherical lens")—proved a challenge, especially as ILM had a deadline to deliver the 3D plates to the companies responsible for the 3D conversion.[[13]](#cite_note-awn-15) ILM made 600 3D shots, and Digital Domain had under 200,[[75]](#cite_note-ILMpanel-77) while Legend3D, the lead 3D conversion company of the film, completed 78 minutes of work on the film and finalized the work of approximately 40 minutes of challenging non-visual effects and 38 minutes of visual effects shots.[[80]](#cite_note-Legend3-D-82)

Composer [Steve Jablonsky](/wiki/Steve_Jablonsky "Steve Jablonsky"), who had before collaborated with Bay on *The Island* and the first two *Transformers* films, returned to compose the *Dark of the Moon* score. Jablonsky's musical score was lauded by critics and fans. The score was released on June 24, 2011, five days before the actual release of the film.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

> Although a bit more score-heavy this time around, the soundtrack is certainly featured and we not only rejoin characters we have come to know, but some familiar artists who are back again as well.

— Allison Loring, reviewing the film's soundtrack[[82]](#cite_note-84)

The album was originally set for release on June 28, 2011, but [Amazon.com](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") listed the album as unavailable while the album was still being listed for release during the week of the film's global release.[[83]](#cite_note-AmazonRelease-85) It was available for download on Amazon on June 30, 2011,[[83]](#cite_note-AmazonRelease-85) and the score currently features 17 pre-recorded tracks that are featured in the final film. The score's length is approximately 59:47.[[84]](#cite_note-86) The album for the film was released on June 14, 2011. It consists of singles produced by different artists and bands, and rock tracks. American [rock](/wiki/Rock_(music) "Rock (music)") band [Linkin Park](/wiki/Linkin_Park "Linkin Park") composed the lead single for the film, "[Iridescent](/wiki/Iridescent_(song) "Iridescent (song)")", as they did with the first two films: "[What I've Done](/wiki/What_I%27ve_Done "What I've Done")" was used in the 2007 film and they composed "[New Divide](/wiki/New_Divide "New Divide")" for the 2009 film. The music video for "Iridescent" was directed by [Joe Hahn](/wiki/Joe_Hahn "Joe Hahn").[[85]](#cite_note-87) Two other singles were released specifically for the soundtrack, "[Monster](/wiki/Monster_(Paramore_song) "Monster (Paramore song)")" by [Paramore](/wiki/Paramore "Paramore")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and "[All That You Are](/wiki/All_That_You_Are_(Goo_Goo_Dolls_song) "All That You Are (Goo Goo Dolls song)")" by the [Goo Goo Dolls](/wiki/Goo_Goo_Dolls "Goo Goo Dolls"). Several other unreleased songs make their debut on the album, including "The Pessimist" by [Stone Sour](/wiki/Stone_Sour "Stone Sour") and "The Bottom" by [Staind](/wiki/Staind "Staind"). [My Chemical Romance](/wiki/My_Chemical_Romance "My Chemical Romance")'s song "[The Only Hope for Me Is You](/wiki/The_Only_Hope_for_Me_Is_You "The Only Hope for Me Is You")" also appeared and can also be found on their fourth studio album *[Danger Days: The True Lives of the Fabulous Killjoys](/wiki/Danger_Days:_The_True_Lives_of_the_Fabulous_Killjoys "Danger Days: The True Lives of the Fabulous Killjoys")* and on the soundtrack. The song was not exclusively for the movie and was featured in the credits.[[86]](#cite_note-88) The [Black Veil Brides](/wiki/Black_Veil_Brides "Black Veil Brides")' song "Set the World on Fire" is also included.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Marketing")]

[![A bus with Optimus Prime and Chinese characters printed on its side drives across a street.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/41/Transformers3Promotion.jpg/250px-Transformers3Promotion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transformers3Promotion.jpg)

An advertisement for the film on a bus in Hong Kong

Most of the characters returned for [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro")'s new [toyline](/wiki/Toyline "Toyline"), which was released on May 16, 2011.[[87]](#cite_note-89) In October 2010, *[Entertainment Tonight](/wiki/Entertainment_Tonight "Entertainment Tonight")* previewed the [behind-the-scenes](/wiki/Making-of "Making-of") filming in Chicago.[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) A two-minute [teaser trailer](/wiki/Teaser_trailer "Teaser trailer") was announced on November 27,[[90]](#cite_note-92) and was posted to the Internet on December 9, 2010.[[91]](#cite_note-93) A 30-second [television advertisement](/wiki/Television_advertisement "Television advertisement") for the film aired during [Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV") on [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") TV channel on February 6, 2011.[[92]](#cite_note-94) The first full [theatrical trailer](/wiki/Theatrical_trailer "Theatrical trailer") was released on April 28, 2011.[[93]](#cite_note-95) The very first [video clip](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video") was released on May 18.[[94]](#cite_note-96) A second clip was released the next day on May 19.[[95]](#cite_note-97) The North American promotional costs came to approximately $75 million.[[6]](#cite_note-Fernandez-7)[[96]](#cite_note-98)

### Novelizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Novelizations")]

In May 2011, the novelization, junior novel, and graphic novel of *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* were released. Both the novel and the graphic novel featured Skids and Mudflap as supporting characters, but the characters were missing from the junior novel. The graphic novel made reference to several Autobots from the [IDW Publishing](/wiki/IDW_Publishing "IDW Publishing") tie-in comics who died in the stories between *Revenge of the Fallen* and *Dark of the Moon*.[[97]](#cite_note-99) Another novel, written by [Peter David](/wiki/Peter_David "Peter David"), was published on May 24, 2011, and was released only paperback. Though it is slightly different from the film, the novel still pertains to the topic and synopsis of the film it is based on in the outcome of the final battle. The novel features about 400 pages and is published by [Del Rey Books](/wiki/Del_Rey_Books "Del Rey Books"). Its synopsis is:

> All humankind was watching that day in 1969. And yet only a handful knew the real mission behind America's triumph in the space race: to explore the alien ship that has crashed on the far side of the moon. Decades later, scientists are still struggling to understand the technology found on board—though with the treacherous Decepticons after it, a powerful force must be at stake. The only hope of averting a crisis is to reawaken Sentinel Prime, the long-lost leader of the Autobots—but who knows what else remains in the shadows, hidden from man and machine?[[98]](#cite_note-100)

### Video game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Video game")]

Main article: [*Transformers: Dark of the Moon* (video game)](/wiki/Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon_(video_game) "Transformers: Dark of the Moon (video game)")

On June 14, 2011, [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") published [a video game based on the film](/wiki/Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon_(video_game) "Transformers: Dark of the Moon (video game)") for [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"), [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3"), [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii"), [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS") and [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS"). The versions for Nintendo's consoles were developed by [Behaviour Interactive](/wiki/Behaviour_Interactive "Behaviour Interactive"),[[99]](#cite_note-101) while the versions for the rest of the consoles were developed by [High Moon Studios](/wiki/High_Moon_Studios "High Moon Studios"), who had previously developed *[Transformers: War for Cybertron](/wiki/Transformers:_War_for_Cybertron "Transformers: War for Cybertron")*. [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") released the game *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* on June 28, 2011, for [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") [Symbian](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian") smartphones, [Apple](/wiki/Apple_(company) "Apple (company)") products [iPod Touch](/wiki/IPod_Touch "IPod Touch"), [iPhones](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone"), and [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad")[[100]](#cite_note-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103) and [Research In Motion](/wiki/Research_In_Motion "Research In Motion")'s [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry "BlackBerry") devices.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

### Theatrical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Theatrical")]

*Transformers: Dark of the Moon* first premiered at the [Moscow International Film Festival](/wiki/Moscow_International_Film_Festival "Moscow International Film Festival") on June 23, 2011. [Linkin Park](/wiki/Linkin_Park "Linkin Park") performed a special outdoor concert in [Red Square](/wiki/Red_Square "Red Square") in Moscow on the same night in celebration of the event.[[2]](#cite_note-Premiere-3) Initially scheduled to be released on July 1, 2011, the release was brought forward to June 29, 2011.[[4]](#cite_note-datechange-5) It was announced in November 2010 that unlike *Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen*, no scenes in the film were shot with [IMAX 3-D](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") cameras.[[103]](#cite_note-105)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Home media")]

During Hasbro Investor Day, it was announced that the [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") Disc would be released in the fourth quarter of 2011.[[104]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-106) The [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") home release for the film was released on September 30, 2011,[[105]](#cite_note-107) with a Blu-ray 3D version of the film slated for release in "the coming months".[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109) The first home release was criticized for the lack of bonus features.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

A [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") exclusive edition of *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* also was released on September 30, 2011.[[109]](#cite_note-111) The [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") DVD and Blu-ray Discs of *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* was released on November 28, 2011.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

In North America, it sold 716,218 DVD units (equivalent of $13,565,169) in its first week, topping the weekly DVD chart.[[111]](#cite_note-113) As of February 26, 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit), it has sold 2,829,285 DVD units (equivalent of $48,058,979).[[112]](#cite_note-114) It also topped the Blu-ray charts on the same week and it has sold 2,381,657 Blu-ray units (earning $50,934,911) by October 23, 2011.[[113]](#cite_note-115) The Blu-ray 3D release of the film was released on January 31, 2012, in NTSC regions and on February 13, 2012, in PAL regions.[[114]](#cite_note-116)

*Transformers: Dark of the Moon* was released on 4K UHD Blu-ray on October 30, 2017, in PAL regions and December 5, 2017, in NTSC regions.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

The film grossed $113.9 million in home sales.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Box office")]

#### Worldwide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Worldwide")]

*Transformers: Dark of the Moon* had grossed $352.3 million in North America, and $771.4 million in other territories, for a worldwide total of $1.123 billion.[[117]](#cite_note-boxofficemojo1-119) It was the [fifth highest-grossing film of all time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films "List of highest-grossing films") and the [second-highest-grossing film of 2011](/wiki/2011_in_film#Highest-grossing_films "2011 in film").[[118]](#cite_note-120) On August 3, 2011, the film crossed the $1 billion mark,[[119]](#cite_note-121) making it the second Paramount film to do so, along with *[Titanic](/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film) "Titanic (1997 film)")*. Its worldwide opening weekend ($382.4 million) is the fourth-largest ever and the largest for Paramount[[120]](#cite_note-122) It set an [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") worldwide opening-weekend record with $23.1 million (first surpassed by *[Deathly Hallows – Part 2](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Deathly_Hallows_%E2%80%93_Part_2 "Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 2")*).[[121]](#cite_note-123) It reached $400 million (6 days), $500 million (9 days), $600 million (12 days) and $700 million (16 days) in record time, but lost all records to *Deathly Hallows – Part 2*.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

#### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: North America")]

The film opened in 4,088 theaters including a then-record total of 2,789 3-D locations. It made $5.5 million during Tuesday 9 p.m. showings, $8 million during midnight showings[[123]](#cite_note-125) and $37.7 million on its opening day (Wednesday)—including Tuesday showings. This was the sixth-best opening Wednesday.[[124]](#cite_note-126) However, all these figures were lower than *Revenge of the Fallen*.[[125]](#cite_note-127) On Thursday, it earned $21.5 million, falling 43%, an improvement from its predecessor's Wednesday-to-Thursday decline. It grossed $33.0 million on Friday totaling $97.8 million. Its 3-D share accounted for 60% of its gross, which was atypical due to the downturn in 3D attendance in North America. For its three-day opening weekend (Friday-to-Sunday), it grossed $97.9 million, topping the box office ahead of *[Cars 2](/wiki/Cars_2 "Cars 2")*.[[126]](#cite_note-128) It achieved the third-largest opening weekend of 2011,[[127]](#cite_note-129) the fourth-largest opening weekend in July,[[128]](#cite_note-130) the fifth-largest opening weekend for a film not released on Friday[[129]](#cite_note-131) and the second-largest five-day gross for a film opening on Wednesday.[[117]](#cite_note-boxofficemojo1-119) It set records for the 3-day ($97.9 million)[[130]](#cite_note-132) and 4-day ($115.9 million) [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)") weekend, surpassing *[Spider-Man 2](/wiki/Spider-Man_2 "Spider-Man 2")*'s record in both cases ($88.2 million and $115.8 million, respectively) until both records were broken with the release of *[Minions: The Rise of Gru](/wiki/Minions:_The_Rise_of_Gru "Minions: The Rise of Gru")* in 2022.[[131]](#cite_note-133)[[132]](#cite_note-134) It retained first place on its second weekend, dropping 52% to $47.1 million.[[133]](#cite_note-135) Closing on October 13, 2011, with $352.4 million, it is the second-highest-grossing film of 2011[[134]](#cite_note-136) and the second-highest-grossing film in the [franchise](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series) "Transformers (film series)").[[135]](#cite_note-137)[[136]](#cite_note-138)

#### Outside North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Outside North America")]

The film grossed $32.5 million on its opening day, pacing 38% ahead of its predecessor. Including some early Tuesday previews, it earned $36.6 million in one-and-a-half days,[[137]](#cite_note-opening_day_THR-139) and by Thursday its international total reached $66 million.[[138]](#cite_note-140) By the end of its first weekend, it had earned $219.8 million, which stands as the fifth-largest opening weekend of all time overseas and the largest for [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures").[[139]](#cite_note-141) Its foreign launch was 57% ahead of that of *Revenge of the Fallen* ($139.6 million). 70% of its grosses came from 3-D (a higher 3-D share than *Pirates 4*'s 66%).[[140]](#cite_note-MojoNews-142) Don Harris, general manager of distribution for Paramount, commented on the results of *Dark of the Moon*: "If we hadn't chosen to debut the movie later in Japan and China, we probably would have had the all-time record."[[141]](#cite_note-143) The film topped the box office outside North America for two weekends in a row.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

In China, its highest-grossing market after North America, the film set records for an opening day with $15.9 million, a single day with $17.4 million (overtaken by *[Journey to the West: Conquering the Demons](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West:_Conquering_the_Demons "Journey to the West: Conquering the Demons")*)[[143]](#cite_note-145) and an opening weekend with $46.8 million ($62.7 million with previews).[[144]](#cite_note-146) The latter was taken from *Avatar* ($42.0 million).[[145]](#cite_note-147) The opening weekend record, when including previews, was surpassed by *[Titanic 3D](/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film)#3D_re-release "Titanic (1997 film)")* ($74.2 million).[[146]](#cite_note-148) *Dark of the Moon* ended its run with $167.95 million, marking the second-highest-grossing film of 2011 (after *Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 2*).[[147]](#cite_note-149) Besides China, it broke the opening-day record in Russia and South Korea;[[137]](#cite_note-opening_day_THR-139) the single-day record in Hong Kong;[[148]](#cite_note-150) and the opening-weekend record in South Korea,[[149]](#cite_note-151) Hong Kong, Malaysia, Taiwan, Thailand, Singapore, the UAE, the Philippines and Peru (the last three records were surpassed by *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film)#Box_office "The Avengers (2012 film)")*).[[140]](#cite_note-MojoNews-142)[[150]](#cite_note-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153) Following China in total earnings were South Korea ($69.1 million) and Japan ($54.2 million).[[152]](#cite_note-154)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film has an approval rating of 35% based on reviews from 259 critics and an average rating of 5/10. The site's critical consensus reads, "Its special effects—and 3D shots—are undeniably impressive, but they aren't enough to fill up its loud, bloated running time, or mask its thin, indifferent script."[[153]](#cite_note-tomatoes-155) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film has a score of 42 out of 100 based on 37 critics, indicating "mixed or average reviews".[[154]](#cite_note-metacritic-156) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A" on an A+ to F scale.[[155]](#cite_note-CinemaScore-157)[[156]](#cite_note-158)

[Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") gave the film one out of four stars, criticizing its visuals, plot, characters, and dialogue.[[157]](#cite_note-159) [Richard Roeper](/wiki/Richard_Roeper "Richard Roeper") likewise panned the film, giving it a D and responded that "rarely has a movie had less of a soul and less interesting characters."[[158]](#cite_note-160) A.O. Scott in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote "I can't decide if this movie is so spectacularly, breathtakingly dumb as to induce stupidity in anyone who watches, or so brutally brilliant that it disarms all reason. What's the difference?"[[159]](#cite_note-ScottNYT-161)

Several critics felt that Shia LaBeouf and Rosie Huntington-Whiteley's performances were ineffective. [Peter Travers](/wiki/Peter_Travers "Peter Travers") of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* gave the film 0 stars, the same rating that he had given to *Revenge of the Fallen*, and stated the two actors "couldn't be duller".[[160]](#cite_note-162) Tirdad Derakhshani of *[The Philadelphia Inquirer](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Inquirer "The Philadelphia Inquirer")* stated that LaBeouf "plays Witwicky as if he had a ferocious case of attention deficit disorder. After two films, his fidgeting isn't cute anymore."[[161]](#cite_note-163) [James Berardinelli](/wiki/James_Berardinelli "James Berardinelli") of ReelViews wrote that LaBeouf "has sunk to greater levels of incompetence here. It's hard to call his posturing and screaming 'acting'."[[162]](#cite_note-164) Jason Solomons of *The Observer* wrote "[W]e're first introduced to [Huntington-Whiteley] via a close-up of her bum, segueing straight from the film's opening sequence and titles on to the pert buttocks and underwear of our heroine", and that her English posh girl accent "renders her practically unintelligible when surrounded by American accents and falling masonry."[[163]](#cite_note-Solomons-165) Much of the criticism towards Rosie Huntington-Whiteley compared her in an unfavorable light to Megan Fox. [Lou Lumenick](/wiki/Lou_Lumenick "Lou Lumenick") of the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* wrote that her "'acting' makes ... Megan Fox look like [Meryl Streep](/wiki/Meryl_Streep "Meryl Streep") in comparison."[[164]](#cite_note-166) Huntington-Whitely was later nominated for the [Golden Raspberry Award for Worst Supporting Actress](/wiki/Golden_Raspberry_Award_for_Worst_Supporting_Actress "Golden Raspberry Award for Worst Supporting Actress") for her performance, but lost to [David Spade](/wiki/David_Spade "David Spade") for *[Jack and Jill](/wiki/Jack_and_Jill_(2011_film) "Jack and Jill (2011 film)")*.[[165]](#cite_note-167)

> For good or ill, Bay is the soul of a new machine, the poet of post-human cinema, the CEO of Hollywood's military-entertainment complex.

— [Richard Corliss](/wiki/Richard_Corliss "Richard Corliss"), in his review of the film for *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine[[166]](#cite_note-168)

Steve Prokopy of [Ain't It Cool News](/wiki/Ain%27t_It_Cool_News "Ain't It Cool News") found the film to be better than the first two.[[167]](#cite_note-169) Jim Vejvoda of [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") gave the film a score of seven out of ten, also stating that it was the best of the franchise.[[168]](#cite_note-170) [E!](/wiki/E! "E!") Online graded the film a B+ while noting if this film is truly the end of a trilogy, its main antagonists should have played more of a part.[[169]](#cite_note-171) Website Daily Bhaskar also praised the film, rating it three and a half out of five stars, citing it as an improvement on the previous film, and writing that it "gives fans something to cheer about."[[170]](#cite_note-172)

Many reviews praised the film's special effects and aggressive use of 3-D. After previewing a partial, unfinished cut of the film, Kofi Outlaw of Screen Rant declared that Bay had created the best 3-D experience since James Cameron's *Avatar*.[[171]](#cite_note-173) Neil Schneider of Meant to be Seen, a website focused on stereoscopic 3-D gaming and entertainment, remarked that "while *Transformers: Dark of the Moon* had the scrapings of a really good story, this 3-D movie was shot with a 2-D script." On the topic of 3-D, Schneider said "*Transformers 3* was a mix of native stereoscopic 3-D camera capturing and 2-D/3-D conversion (as a 3-D tool), and most was done very well." He added, "At a minimum, *Transformers 3* demonstrates that fast cutting sequences are indeed possible and practical in stereoscopic 3-D. More than that, it was a comfortable experience and helped exemplify great use of stereoscopic 3-D with CGI live-action and CGI digital characters. That said, I think they still could have taken it much further."[[172]](#cite_note-174)

[Charlie Jane Anders](/wiki/Charlie_Jane_Anders "Charlie Jane Anders") of [Jezebel](/wiki/Jezebel_(website) "Jezebel (website)") believed that some elements of the film were deliberate self-references to Michael Bay's own sense of under-appreciation after the backlash to the second film: "After a few hours of seeing Shia get dissed, overlooked and mistreated, the message becomes clear: Shia, as always, is a stand-in for Michael Bay. And Bay is showing us just what it felt like to deal with the ocean of Haterade—the snarking, the [Razzie Award](/wiki/Golden_Raspberry_Awards "Golden Raspberry Awards"), the mean reviews—that *Revenge of the Fallen* unleashed." She went on to say that the film's frequent, often jarring shifts in tone were an intentional endorsement of Michael Bay's own filmmaking style. "Tone is for single-purpose machines. Consistency is for Decepticons. Michael Bay's ideal movie shifts from action movie to teen comedy to political drama with the same well-lubricated ease that his cars become men. By the time you've finished watching, you will speak Michael Bay's cinematic language."[[173]](#cite_note-175)

## Sequels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Transformers:_Dark_of_the_Moon&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Sequels")]

Further information: [*Transformers* (film series)](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series) "Transformers (film series)")

Despite previous discussions regarding Bay's departure of the *Transformers* film series, he returned to direct a sequel, and development of the follow-up began afterward.[[205]](#cite_note-207) Di Bonaventura described the project as an "evolution", with another set of cast members and a different story.[[206]](#cite_note-208) *[Transformers: Age of Extinction](/wiki/Transformers:_Age_of_Extinction "Transformers: Age of Extinction")* (2014) received a less enthusiastic response and achieved similar financial success as *Dark of the Moon*, becoming the [highest-grossing film of 2014](/wiki/2014_in_film#Highest-grossing_films "2014 in film").[[207]](#cite_note-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210)[[209]](#cite_note-BOMWorldwide-211) It was followed by *[Transformers: The Last Knight](/wiki/Transformers:_The_Last_Knight "Transformers: The Last Knight")* (2017), which became a [box-office bomb](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb") and lost the studio an estimated $100 million.[[210]](#cite_note-212)[[211]](#cite_note-CEO-213) The series narrative was expanded in two films that preceded the first five: *[Bumblebee](/wiki/Bumblebee_(film) "Bumblebee (film)")* (2018) shows the title character arriving on Earth; and *[Transformers: Rise of the Beasts](/wiki/Transformers:_Rise_of_the_Beasts "Transformers: Rise of the Beasts")* (2023) focuses on the conflict between the Autobots and the [Maximals](/wiki/Maximals "Maximals"), and the Terrorcons.[[212]](#cite_note-214)
